headline: With the ROAD Act now law, the federal housing pipeline shifts to rulemaking and a queue of tax-credit and anti-institutional-buyer bills that will define the next cycle.
paragraphs: The immediate post-enactment watch on P.L. 119-101 (the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, signed July 11) is HUD rulemaking: the statutory changes to FHA loan limits, HOME eligibility, and environmental review streamlining take effect as implementing guidance is issued, and developers and lenders with live FHA pipelines should stress-test underwriting assumptions against the new limits before guidance lands. Meanwhile, Florida's enacted HJR 1-F goes to the November ballot — the constitutional amendments on assessment-increase caps and the higher homestead exemption will reshape NOI assumptions on Florida income properties if approved, and lenders underwriting Florida portfolios should begin scenario-planning around the revenue-cap provisions affecting county and municipal budgets.

Three federal bills directly affecting deal economics remain pending and bear close watching. The LIHTC reform package (H.R. 2725) would expand annual credit allocations and strengthen the 9% credit — a material improvement to affordable housing deal feasibility if it moves through Ways and Means. The new workforce housing tax credit (H.R. 8626) would open a middle-income financing lane that currently has no federal subsidy analog. And the office-to-residential conversion investment credit (H.R. 2410) would give a federal affordability requirement to conversion deals while unlocking tax equity for non-historic buildings. On the risk side, the anti-institutional-buyer bill (S.B. 3937, amending the Sherman Act to bar large entities from purchasing single-family homes) and the companion proposals prohibiting Chinese-affiliated acquisitions (H.R. 4706, S. 2258) continue moving through committee; any of these, if enacted, would require immediate deal-structure review for SFR acquirers and capital with foreign beneficial ownership.

State-level, the most advanced items to close-watch are New York's 485-x technical fix bills (S10345 and S10500), the good cause eviction corrections (S08612), and the Deed Protection Act (A09554, passed Assembly), all of which have underwriting implications for active New York deals. Pennsylvania's anti-predatory listing-contract bill (H.B. 986) passed the House and sits in Senate Urban Affairs — it follows the same trajectory as New York's S06361, which was signed in December 2025, and a parallel enactment in Pennsylvania would affect commission-structure disclosures statewide. The first-time homebuyer savings account bill (H.B. 818) is also in Senate committee and, if enacted, would modestly expand the buyer pool by improving down-payment accumulation for first-time purchasers.
